extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property requires that flipping slurred_speech from false to true (other inputs fixed) never lowers acuity. Enum order: self_care < routine < urgent < emergent.

Consider the baseline input dizziness=true, syncope=true, slurred_speech=FALSE, hemoptysis=false:
- r1 (p2, dizziness) fires -> urgent
- r2 needs slurred_speech -> not fired
- r3 needs hemoptysis -> not fired
- r4 (p3, syncope) fires -> emergent
Highest-priority matching rule is r4 (p3), so acuity = emergent.

Now flip slurred_speech to TRUE, keeping dizziness=true, syncope=true, hemoptysis=false:
- r1 (p2) fires -> urgent
- r2 (p100, dizziness AND syncope AND slurred_speech) fires -> self_care
- r3 not fired
- r4 (p3) fires -> emergent
Highest-priority matching rule is now r2 (p100), so acuity = self_care.

Setting slurred_speech true changes acuity from emergent (highest) to self_care (lowest), a decrease. The high-priority rule r2 (priority 100) overrides the emergent verdict from r4 (priority 3) precisely when slurred_speech becomes true. This violates the monotonicity property. Counterexample: dizziness=true, syncope=true, slurred_speech=true, hemoptysis=false (baseline = same with slurred_speech=false yields emergent).
